statement: Denmark's agricultural and food sector is one of the largest in the EU relative to the country's size. Agricultural and food products account for approximately 20–25% of Denmark's total goods exports. Denmark is one of the world's largest exporters of pork (approximately 28 million pigs slaughtered annually for a population of 5.9 million), dairy products (Arla Foods is one of the world's largest dairy cooperatives), and processed food ingredients. The sector directly employs approximately 185,000 people (about 6% of total employment). Denmark's agricultural success within the EU is often cited in Iceland's EU debate as evidence that small Nordic countries can thrive under the Common Agricultural Policy — though Denmark's agricultural sector (temperate arable and livestock farming) differs fundamentally from Iceland's (sub-Arctic pastoral farming dominated by sheep and dairy).
source_name: Danish Agriculture & Food Council — Facts & Figures; Statistics Denmark
caveats: The 20–25% figure varies depending on whether processed food products (e.g., brewing, confectionery) are included alongside primary agricultural products. Denmark's agricultural success predates EU membership and is also attributable to its cooperative tradition, advanced agricultural research, and temperate climate. The comparison with Iceland is limited by fundamentally different agricultural conditions.

related_entries.statement: During Iceland's EU accession negotiations (2010–2013), the agriculture chapter (Chapter 11) was one of the most politically sensitive negotiating chapters and was never opened for negotiations before the process was suspended in May 2013. Analytical screening of Chapter 11 was completed by the European Commission in 2011, identifying significant gaps between Iceland's agricultural policy and the EU acquis. Key issues included: Iceland's high tariff protection (incompatible with the EU customs union), the absence of CAP-compatible direct payment schemes, differences in food safety and veterinary standards, and the need to fundamentally restructure agricultural support. Of the 35 negotiating chapters, Iceland opened 27 and provisionally closed 11 — an unprecedented pace enabled by existing EEA compliance. However, the chapters not opened included the most politically difficult: Chapter 11 (agriculture), Chapter 13 (fisheries), Chapter 17 (economic and monetary policy), and Chapter 33 (financial and budgetary provisions). These same chapters would be the focus of any resumed negotiations.

UK goods exports to the EU have fallen significantly since Brexit. In 2024, UK goods exports to the EU were £177 billion, approximately 18% below their 2019 level in real terms, having previously exceeded £215 billion in 2017, 2018, and 2019. Services exports to the EU performed better, rising 19% above 2019 levels in real terms. The UK-EU Trade and Cooperation Agreement (TCA) provides zero-tariff, zero-quota access but introduced substantial non-tariff barriers including customs declarations, rules of origin requirements, sanitary and phytosanitary checks, and regulatory divergence. The UK's share of EU imports has declined relative to pre-Brexit trends. Total UK exports of goods and services to the EU were £358 billion in 2024, representing 41% of all UK exports.
